perturbation.original_text: The parties agree to inform all personnel receiving the proprietary information of the terms of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties may inform personnel receiving the proprietary information of the terms of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agree to inform' to 'may inform' weakens the requirement to notify personnel about the NDA terms. This could allow a company to claim ignorance if an employee breaches the NDA, potentially affecting liability and enforceability. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which is a common law principle in contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied duty of good faith requires parties to a contract to act honestly and fairly. By weakening the obligation to inform personnel, the modified text allows for potential bad faith conduct, contradicting this duty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates informing personnel ('agree to inform'), while the changed text makes it discretionary ('may inform'). This weakens the obligation to inform personnel. The Law Explanation states that this weakening allows for potential bad faith conduct, contradicting the implied duty of good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that every contract imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Therefore, by making the informing of personnel optional, the changed text contradicts the obligation of good faith, as it could be argued that not informing personnel is acting in bad faith.

perturbation.original_text: All materials transmitted from one party to the other and containing proprietary information shall remain the property of the transferor and shall be returned upon request, and receipt of such materials agrees not to reproduce sa id materials without written consent of the transferor of the materials.
perturbation.changed_text: All materials transmitted from one party to the other and containing proprietary information shall be returned upon request.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the clause prohibiting reproduction of the materials without written consent creates a loophole. This omission violates copyright laws if the materials are copyrighted. Even if not explicitly copyrighted, it violates trade secret laws that protect against unauthorized use or disclosure of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S.C. § 106 grants copyright holders the exclusive rights to reproduce their work. By removing the prohibition on reproduction, the contract potentially allows the receiving party to infringe on these rights if the materials are copyrighted, even if the contract intends to protect proprietary information.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 17 / CHAPTER 1 / § 106 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 17 USC 106 : Exclusive rights in copyrighted works Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 17-COPYRIGHTS CHAPTER 1-SUBJECT MATTER AND SCOPE OF COPYRIGHT Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date §106. Exclusive rights in copyrighted works Subject to sections 107 through 122, the owner of copyright under this title has the exclusive rights to do and to authorize any of the following: (1) to reproduce the copyrighted work in copies or phonorecords; (2) to prepare derivative works based upon the copyrighted work; (3) to distribute copies or phonorecords of the copyrighted work to the public by sale or other transfer of ownership, or by rental,
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly prohibited the reproduction of materials without written consent, which aligns with copyright protections detailed in 17 U.S.C. § 106, as highlighted in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 2. The changed text removes this prohibition, potentially allowing the receiving party to reproduce copyrighted work, thus infringing on the copyright holder's exclusive rights. This directly contradicts the core intent of copyright law to protect the reproduction of copyrighted works.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, it may be necessary for the transmission of information proprietary to one of the parties to the other party during cooperation between parties; and WHEREAS, the parties desire to protect said transmitted proprietary inform ation while ensuring the free flow of information between the parties necessary for said cooperative effort
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, it may be necessary for the transmission of information proprietary to one of the parties to the other party during cooperation between parties; and WHEREAS, the parties desire to ensure the free flow of information between the parties necessary for said cooperative effort
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'the parties desire to protect said transmitted proprietary information' weakens the intent to protect the proprietary information. This can be problematic because under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), one of the requirements for a misappropriation claim is that the owner of the trade secret took reasonable measures to protect its secrecy. By omitting this explicit intent, the agreement is less able to rely on trade secret protection and opens the door to a party claiming the information was not treated as a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA defines trade secrets and provides remedies for misappropriation. A key element of a trade secret claim is that the owner took reasonable measures to protect its secrecy.  The omission of the stated desire to protect the information undermines the argument that reasonable measures were taken, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection under UTSA.
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'the parties desire to protect said transmitted proprietary information' directly undermines the 'reasonable measures' requirement for trade secret protection under UTSA. The Perturbation and Law Explanations both emphasize that this omission weakens the ability to claim trade secret protection, as a critical expression of intent to protect the information has been removed. Although there are no scraped snippets available, based on the explanation provided, the modified text makes it significantly harder to argue that the information was treated as a trade secret.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree not to use the proprietary information to the detriment of the transmitting party. The parties also agree to maintain any such proprietary in formation received from the other party in confidence and agree not to disclose any such information to persons not having a need to know the information consistent with the purpose of this agreement until information is publicly disclosed by the party tra nsmitting the information.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree not to disclose any such information to persons not having a need to know the information consistent with the purpose of this agreement until information is publicly disclosed by the party tra nsmitting the information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'The parties agree not to use the proprietary information to the detriment of the transmitting party. The parties also agree to maintain any such proprietary information received from the other party in confidence' weakens the protection afforded to the disclosing party. It no longer explicitly states the receiving party cannot use the information to hurt the disclosing party, and also fails to state that the proprietary information should be kept confidential. Trade secret law generally requires a demonstration that the information was kept confidential and that there was an agreement to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (First) of Torts § 757 provides the common law basis for trade secret protection. It requires, among other things, that the information be secret and that the receiving party be subject to a duty or obligation not to disclose or use the information. The removal of the statement that the receiving party will keep the information confidential undermines a potential trade secret claim, contradicting § 757.

CT@Z>d YB, BPCH @[BFW tA!h z}
perturbation.justification: The removal of the clause regarding the use and confidentiality of proprietary information directly undermines trade secret protection, which relies on maintaining secrecy and establishing a duty to keep the information confidential. The Perturbation Explanation states that 'It no longer explicitly states the receiving party cannot use the information to hurt the disclosing party, and also fails to state that the proprietary information should be kept confidential'. Also, the Law Explanation states that Restatement (First) of Torts § 757 requires that 'the receiving party be subject to a duty or obligation not to disclose or use the information.' The modified text, by omitting these explicit protections, would likely fail legal scrutiny under trade secret law, creating a clear legal contradiction.
